---
title: "State tax revenue volatility"
author: "Don Boyd"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_notebook: 
    df_print: paged
    fig_height: 6
    fig_width: 8
    toc: yes
    toc_float: yes
    number_sections: yes
  html_document:
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: '3'
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

<!-- <style> -->
<!-- body .main-container { -->
<!--   max-width: 1500px; -->
<!-- } -->
<!-- </style> -->

<!-- Google drive -->
<!-- https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1fM7HIP7qrKbIdndF_wQ30WxVb1j_Cq06 -->


```{r runall, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE} 
# When we want a final report, run the following code selectively "by hand" (interactively)
# -- NEVER using Knit with eval=TRUE
# note <br> breaks line for html output but \n should break for pdf; also could try |

rmdfn <- "./Pew_Tax_Revenue_Volatility.rmd" # this file
outfn <- paste0("tax_volatility_", format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d"), ".html")
rmarkdown::render(rmdfn, output_format="html_document", output_file=outfn)

# library("RCurl")
# ftpUpload(outfn, "ftp://kffrig:boyd4812@files.000webhost.com/public_html/KFF_RIG_MedicaidCuts_new.html")

# Note may be safest to fully exit RStudio and restart it before running whole thing. Otherwise knitr may get confused
# and include repetitive information in the output html file.

```


```{r setup, include=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE, include=FALSE, eval=FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```


```{r css, echo=FALSE}
# blockquote {
#     padding: 20px;
#     margin: 0 0 20px;
#     font-size: 14px;
#     border-left: 5px solid #eee;
# }

```


<!--
Workflow notes

# code folding ----
#   alt-L, alt-shift-L  one section
#   alt-O, alt-shift-O  all sections
#   ctrl-D run current code section (remapped from Ctrl-alt-T)

always_allow_html: yes

  html_document:
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: '2'
    df_print: paged
    number_sections: yes

# further notes
# to get it all started:
# renv::init()

# to remove it all:
# renv::deactivate()
# then delete the renv folder

# periodically execute:
# renv::snapshot()

# to see what directory is being used by renv for local package sources, execute:
# renv:::renv_paths_local()

-->


```{r git_workflow, eval=FALSE}
# when starting work from a different computer
# - pull project from remote
# git checkout -b issue-5  # create branch and checkout branch
# Stage local changes, commit, check status:
# git add foo.txt
# git commit --message "A commit message"
# git status

# now merge
# git checkout master
# git merge issue-5

# then push
# git push

```


```{r get_libs_and_functions, eval=TRUE}
# clear objects from the workspace
rm(list=ls(all=TRUE))
source(here::here("r", "libs_base.r"))
source(here::here("r", "libs_ts.r"))
source(here::here("r", "functions.r"))
devtools::session_info()
# devtools::package_info()

```


# Materials for report, organized by topic

## Get previously created data
```{r get_data, include=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
load(file = here::here("data", "general.RData"), verbose=TRUE)
load(file = here::here("data", "econ_national.RData"), verbose=TRUE)
load(file = here::here("data", "econ_state.RData"), verbose=TRUE)
load(file = here::here("data", "taxdata.RData"), verbose=TRUE)

```


## Constants, themes, etc.
```{r include=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
theme_report <- theme_bw() + 
  theme(
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0)
    )


nolegend <- theme(legend.position = "none")
nolegend_title <- theme(legend.title = element_blank())

bluered <- c("blue", "red")  # #a50f15 darkred
color2 <- c("blue", "red")


```


# Scene setting - exploration of patterns

## Year over year change in tax revenue and...

### Nominal GDP


### Inflation
```{r include=FALSE, eval=TRUE}

glimpse(censustax)
 df <- censustax %>%
   filter(stabbr=="US", name=="tottax") %>%
   select(year, tax=value) %>%
   inner_join(gdppi %>% select(year, gdppi), by="year") %>%
   pivot_longer(-year) %>%
   group_by(name) %>%
   mutate(pch=pchya(value, year)) %>%
   ungroup
 

p <- df %>%
  filter(year >= 1955) %>%
  mutate(name=factor(name, levels=c("tax", "gdppi"), labels=c("taxes", "inflation"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, pch, colour=name)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL, breaks=seq(1955, 2025, 5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="% change",
                     breaks=seq(-20, 20, 2)) +
  ggtitle("% change in tax revenue and general price level, United States") +
  scale_colour_manual(values=rev(bluered)) +
  theme_report +
  nolegend_title
p 
ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "gpppi_tax_us.png"), plot = p, width=8, height=6)
 

# df2 %>%
#   filter(year >= 1970) %>%
#   ggplot(aes(year, vol2, colour=name)) +
#   geom_line() +
#   geom_point() +
#   geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
#   scale_x_continuous(name=NULL) +
#   scale_y_continuous(name="volatility (% difference from smoothed trend)",
#                      breaks=seq(-20, 20, 2)) +
#   ggtitle("Volatility of State Government Tax Revenue and Gross Domestic Product, United States",
#           subtitle="Volatility measured as % difference from smoothed trend (HP filter)") +
#   theme_bw()

```




# Alternative volatility measures


```{r include=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
# generate series with two kinds of volatility
nyears <- 30
base <- 100
grate <- .03
set.seed(12)
grvec <- (1 + grate)^(0:(nyears - 1))  # growth vector
noise <- rnorm(nyears, mean=0, sd=1)  # noise vector
shock_pattern <- c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0, .1)

pattern_smooth <- seq(.10, -.10, length.out=10)
# shock_smooth <- rep_len(pattern_smooth, nyears)
shock_smooth <- rep_len(c(pattern_smooth, -pattern_smooth), nyears) # smooth shock goes down then up

shock_smooth

# force our recession shock and random shocks to start at zero - drop last element of each vector
znoise = c(0, head(noise, -1))
zsmooth = c(0, head(shock_smooth, -1))

sum(znoise)
sum(zsmooth)
sum(abs(znoise))
sum(abs(zsmooth))

# force similar noise
ratio <- sum(abs(znoise)) / sum(abs(zsmooth))


df <- tibble(time=1:nyears, # + 2024,
             trend=base*grvec) %>%
  mutate(tnoise=trend * (1 + znoise / ratio),
         tsmooth=trend * (1 - zsmooth)) %>%
  mutate(tnoise = tnoise * sum(trend) / sum(tnoise),
         tsmooth = tsmooth * sum(trend) / sum(tsmooth),
         tnoise=ifelse(time==1, trend, tnoise),
         tsmooth=ifelse(time==1, trend, tsmooth))

# df <- tibble(time=1:nyears, # + 2024,
#              trend=base*grvec) %>%
#   mutate(tnoise=trend * (1 + znoise/25),
#          tsmooth=trend * (1 - zsmooth)* (1 + znoise/50))


dfl <- df %>%
  pivot_longer(-time) %>%
  group_by(name) %>%
  arrange(time) %>%
  mutate(pch=pchya(value, time),
         pch=ifelse(time==1, 0, pch)) %>%
  group_by(time) %>%
  mutate(dpch=(pch - pch[name=="trend"]) /100,
         dtrend=value - value[name=="trend"],
         pdtrend=dtrend / value[name=="trend"]) %>%
  ungroup
glimpse(dfl)

dfl %>%
  group_by(name) %>%
  summarise(value=sum(value))

dfl %>% filter(name=="tnoise")
dfl %>% filter(name=="tsmooth")
# our first panel is trend, random shock, and cyclical shock


```


## Kinds of volatility

States generally face two kinds of volatility: cyclical volatility, often following the business cycle, and random volatility.

In this section we examine three revenue series:

+   Simple trend that grows at 3% per year
+   Revenue source that has pure cyclical volatility
+   Revenue source with random volatility. 


All three revenue series raise the same amount of money over 30 years.

For each of the following graphs, ask:

+   Which revenue source would require a **larger rainy day fund** if a state wanted to protect against long downturns?
+   Which revenue source would be **harder to forecast** if the state were focused on avoiding unanticipated budgfet shortfalls?



```{r eval=TRUE}

fdtrend <- function(type){
  dfl %>%
    filter(name==type) %>%
    mutate(posneg=ifelse(pdtrend < 0, "neg", "pos")) %>%
    ggplot(aes(time, pdtrend)) +
    # geom_line(colour="black") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
    geom_bar(stat="identity", aes(fill = posneg)) + 
    scale_fill_manual(values=rev(bluered)) +
    scale_x_continuous(name="year", breaks=seq(0, 100, 5)) +
    scale_y_continuous(name="% difference from trend",
                       breaks=seq(-.2, .2, .05), 
                       labels=scales::percent_format(accuracy=1),
                       limits=c(-.1, .1)) +
    theme_report +
    nolegend
}


fpdiff <- function(type){
  dfl %>%
    filter(name==type) %>%
    mutate(posneg=ifelse(dpch < 0, "neg", "pos")) %>%
    ggplot(aes(time, dpch)) +
    # geom_line(colour="black") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
    geom_bar(stat = "identity", aes(fill = posneg)) + 
    scale_fill_manual(values=rev(bluered)) +
    scale_x_continuous(name="year", breaks=seq(0, 100, 5)) +
    scale_y_continuous(name="shock % growth minus trend % growth",
                       breaks=seq(-.2, .2, .05), 
                       labels=scales::percent_format(accuracy=1),
                       limits=c(-.1, .1)) +
    theme_report +
    nolegend
}


# ppanels <- grid.arrange(p_cycle_vstrend, p_cycle_vsgrowth, 
#              p_random_vstrend, p_random_vsgrowth, 
#              nrow = 2, top="Two kinds of volatility and two kinds of volatility measures")
# ppanels
# grid::grid.draw(ppanels)


p_cycle_vstrend <- fdtrend("tsmooth") + ggtitle("Shock: cyclical\nMeasure: % deviation from trend revenue")
p_random_vstrend <- fdtrend("tnoise") + ggtitle("Shock: random\nMeasure: % deviation from trend revenue")

p_cycle_vsgrowth <- fpdiff("tsmooth") + ggtitle("Shock: cyclical\nMeasure: shock % growth minus trend % growth")
p_random_vsgrowth <- fpdiff("tnoise") + ggtitle("Shock: random\nMeasure: shock % growth minus trend % growth")


```


### Comparing AMOUNT (level) of revenue relative to trend

We begin by comparing actual revenue to trend revenue. 


```{r eval=TRUE, include=TRUE}
# plot series and percent change
colors = c("black", "blue", "darkgreen")
p1 <- dfl %>%
  mutate(name=factor(name, 
                     levels=c("trend", "tsmooth", "tnoise"),
                     labels=c("trend", "cyclical", "random"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(time, value, colour=name)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="revenue", limits=c(90, 260), labels=scales::dollar) +
  scale_x_continuous(name="year", breaks=seq(0, 40, 5), limits=c(0, 32)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=colors) +
  ggtitle("Two kinds of revenue volatility: pure cyclical and pure random shocks",
          subtitle="All 3 revenue series raise the same amount over 30 years") +
  theme_report +
  nolegend_title
p1
ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "trend_cycle_random_level.png"), plot = p1, width=8, height=6)

# dev.cur()
# dev.off()

```


The two-panel graph below shows actual revenue minus trend revenue for each source. 

+   Which of the two kinds of volatility would subject the state to long periods of shortfalls and might suggest the need for a larger rainy day fund?
+   Which revenue source has greater year-to-year variation and might be more difficult to forecast? How easily can we tell that if we compare to the trend?

In the graph, cyclical volatility subjects the state to long consecutive periods of underperformance and overperformance, suggesting the need for a larger rainy day fund. The deviation-from-trend measure makes this clear.

It's not clear from the graph which form of cyclicality subjects the state to greater year-to-year uncertainty: the deviation-from-trend measure does not tell us much about this.


```{r include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.width=6, fig.height=9}

p_rdf <- grid.arrange(p_cycle_vstrend,
                      p_random_vstrend, 
             nrow = 2, top="Two kinds of volatility, % difference from trend revenue")
grid::grid.draw(p_rdf)

ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "dev_trend.png"), plot = p_rdf, width=8, height=12)


```


## Comparing revenue GROWTH RATES relative to trend growth rates 

Next we compare growth rates of actual revenue to growth rates for trend revenue. 



```{r eval=TRUE, include=TRUE}
# percent change
colors = c("black", "blue", "darkgreen")
p2 <- dfl %>%
  mutate(name=factor(name, 
                     levels=c("trend", "tsmooth", "tnoise"),
                     labels=c("trend", "cyclical", "random"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(time, pch / 100, colour=name)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="% change", labels=scales::percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(name="year", breaks=seq(0, 40, 5), limits=c(0, 32)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=colors) +
  ggtitle("Two kinds of revenue volatility: pure cyclical and pure random shocks",
          subtitle="All 3 revenue series raise the same amount over 30 years") +
  theme_report +
  nolegend_title
p2
ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "trend_cycle_random_pch.png"), plot = p2, width=8, height=6)

```

The two-panel graph below shows actual revenue minus trend revenue for each source. 

+   Which revenue source has greater year-to-year variation and might be more difficult to forecast?
+   Which revenue source would subject the state to long periods of shortfalls and might suggest a larger rainy day fund? How easily can we tell that when we are comparing actual growth rates to trend growth rates?


```{r include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, fig.width=6, fig.height=9}

p_forecast <- grid.arrange(p_cycle_vsgrowth,
                      p_random_vsgrowth, 
             nrow = 2, top="Two kinds of volatility, difference from growth rate in trend revenue")
grid::grid.draw(p_forecast)

ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "dev_pch.png"), plot = p_forecast, width=8, height=12)


```

## Formal measures of volatility

```{r functions, include=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
hp_df <- function(df, vname, smooth=NULL){
  vts <- ts(df[vname])
  vts_hp <- hpfilter(vts, freq=smooth, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
  df %>% 
    mutate(trend=as.numeric(vts_hp$trend),
           cycle=as.numeric(vts_hp$cycle))
}

hptrend <- function(vec, smooth=NULL){
  vts_hp <- hpfilter(ts(vec), freq=smooth, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
  as.numeric(vts_hp$trend)
}

hpdf <- function(data) {
  mod <- hpfilter(data$value, freq=6.25, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
  tibble(trend=as.numeric(mod$trend),
         cycle=as.numeric(mod$cycle))
}


sredf <- function(data) {
  # short-run elasticity
  mod = lm(dlvalue ~ dlgdp, data=data)
  list(mod=mod, coeff=unname(mod$coefficients["dlgdp"]))
}


dl <- function(vec) {
  # change in log
  # ASSUME data are sorted by year and there are no gaps and no zeros
  lvec = log(vec)
  laglvec = c(NA, head(lvec, -1))
  lvec - laglvec
}

```


```{r vcalc}
# df <- censustax %>%
#   arrange(name, year) %>%
#   nest_by(name) %>%
#   filter(year >= 1960, stabbr=="US")
# 
# # %>% mutate(new=hp_df(., "value"))
# 
# censustax %>%
#   filter(year >= 1960, stabbr=="US") %>%
#   arrange(name, year) %>%
#   group_by(name) %>%
#   mutate(hp=hpfilter(value, freq=1200, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE))
# 
# vec <- df %>% filter(name=="tottax") %>% arrange(year) %>% .$value
# res <- hpfilter(vec, freq=1200, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
# str(res)
# typeof(res)
# res$trend[, 1]


# sredf <- function(data) {
#   # short-run elasticity
#   mod = lm(dlvalue ~ dlgdp, data=data)
#   list(mod=mod, coeff=unname(mod$coefficients["dlgdp"]))
# }



df2 <- censustax %>%
  left_join(sgdpfy %>%
              filter(name=="gdp") %>%
              rename(gdp=value) %>%
              select(year, stabbr, gdp),
            by=c("stabbr", "year")) %>%
  arrange(stabbr, name, year) %>% 
  group_by(stabbr, name) %>%
  mutate(pch=value / value[match(year - 1, year)] * 100 - 100,
         pch_rollsd = rollsd(pch, 10),
         dlvalue=dl(value),
         dlgdp=dl(gdp)) %>%
  filter(year >= 1964) %>%  # !(stabbr =="OH" & name=="cit")
  ungroup %>%
  group_nest(stabbr, name) %>%
  mutate(hpf=map(data, safely(hpdf)),
         sre=map(data, safely(sredf)))


df2 %>%
  hoist(sre, coeff=c("result", "coeff"))  # reach within result to get coeff

tmp <- df2 %>%
  filter(stabbr=="NY", name=="iit")
str(tmp$sre)
str(tmp$sre[[1]])
names(tmp$sre[[1]]$result)

coef(tmp$sre[[1]])

tmp$sre[[1]]
names(tmp$sre[[1]])
names(tmp$sre[[1]]$result)
names(tmp$sre[[1]]$result$coeff)

tmp %>%
  select(stabbr, name, sre) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(sre))

tmp %>%
  hoist(sre, coeff=list("result", 1))

tmp %>%
  hoist(sre, coeff=c("result", "coeff"))


tmp %>%
  hoist(sre, "result", "mod", "coefficients", "dlgdp")

tmp %>%
  unnest_wider(sre) %>%
  unnest_wider(result)



tmp %>%
  select(stabbr, name, hpf) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(hpf))



tmp %>%
  ungroup %>%
  hoist(sre,
        mod="mod")


tmp %>%
  ungroup %>%
  hoist(sre,
        # mod="mod",
        coeff="coeff")


names(tmp2$sre[[1]])
tmp2 %>%
  ungroup %>%
  hoist(sre,
        # mod="mod",
        coeff="coeff")


tmp <- df2 %>%
  filter(stabbr=="NY", name=="iit", year >= 1965) %>%
  nest()

tmp2 <- tmp %>%
  mutate(sre=map(data, sredf))
tmp2$sre

mod <- lm(dlvalue ~ dlgdp, data=tmp$data[[1]])
str(mod)
str(mod$coefficients["dlgdp"])
mod$coefficients["dlgdp"]
unname(mod$coefficients["dlgdp"])
str(coef(mod))


# names(belchers$info[[1]])
# belchers %>% hoist(info,
#   name = "name",
#   age = "age",
#   dad = "father",
#   firstborn = list("children", 1L)
# )

names(tmp2$sre[[1]])
tmp2 %>%
  ungroup %>%
  hoist(sre,
        # mod="mod",
        coeff="coeff")

tmp2 %>%
  unnest(sre)
tmp2 %>%
  unnest_wider(sre)

tmp2 %>%
  hoist(sre, "coeff")

tmp2 %>%
  unnest_wider(sre$coeff)


df3 <- df2 %>%
  unnest(cols = c(data, mod)) %>%
  mutate(vol=value / trend * 100 - 100,
         absvol=abs(vol)) %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name) %>%
  mutate(mdn=median(vol),
         absmdn=median(absvol)) %>%
  ungroup

volsum <- df3 %>%
  group_by(name, stabbr) %>%
  summarise(hpmdn=median(abs(vol)),
            hpmean=mean(abs(vol)),
            hpsd=sd(vol),
            pchsd=sd(pch, na.rm=TRUE),
            .groups="drop") %>%
  arrange(-hpmdn)

volsum %>%
  arrange(-hpmdn)

volsum %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US")

volsum %>%
  filter(name=="tottax")

# dev.off()

# US facet plot, one panel for each tax
usdata <- df3 %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US") %>%
  group_by(stabbr, year) %>%
  mutate(comp=vol[name=="tottax"]) %>%
  select(stabbr, name, year, vol, comp) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=c(vol, comp), names_to = "type")

usdata %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=type)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~name, ncol=3, scales="free")

stdata <- df3 %>%
  group_by(name, year) %>%
  mutate(us=vol[stabbr=="US"]) %>%
  select(stabbr, name, year, vol, us) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=c(vol, us), names_to = "geo")

stdata %>%
  filter(name=="tottax") %>%
  filter(stabbr %in% c("CA", "NY", "AL", "MS", "MA", "CT", "OH")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=geo)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~stabbr, ncol=3, scales="free")


sts <- c("AL", "CA", "CT", "FL", "MA", "MS", "NY", "OH","OR", "PA", "TN", "TX", "UT", "VA", "WA")
df3 %>%
  filter(name=="tottax") %>%
  filter(stabbr %in% sts) %>%
  select(stabbr, name, year, absvol, absmdn) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=c(absvol, absmdn), names_to = "measure") %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=measure)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~stabbr, ncol=3, scales="free")

sts <- c("AL", "CA", "CT", "FL", "MA", "MS", "NY", "OH","OR", "PA", "TN", "TX", "UT", "VA", "WA")
df3 %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US") %>%
  select(stabbr, name, year, absvol, absmdn) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=c(absvol, absmdn), names_to = "measure") %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=measure)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~name, ncol=3, scales="free")


```



# Capital gains
```{r eval=TRUE}
# % change in gdp, tax revenue, cg
cgtax <- gdpfy %>%
  full_join(capgains,
            by="year") %>%
  full_join(censustax %>%
              filter(name %in% c("tottax", "iit", "gst")) %>%
              rename(tax=value),
            by="year") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(gdp_pch=pchya(gdp, year),
         cg_pch=pchya(capgains, year),
         tax_pch=pchya(tax, year),
         cggdp=capgains / gdp * 100) %>%
  ungroup
  
```


```{r gdpcgtax_plot}
cgtax %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US") %>%
  select(year, gdp_pch, cg_pch, tax_pch) %>%
  pivot_longer(-year) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=name)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_line() +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  ggtitle("Percent change in GDP, capital gains, and tax revenue for the United States",
          subtitle = "Not adjusted for inflation")
  
```


```{r cggdp_plot, eval=TRUE, include=TRUE, fig.height=6, fig.width=6}
# fix to use calendar year??
rec <- recessions %>%
  filter(rec_year > 1953)

anno <- cgtax %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US") %>%
  mutate(cggdp=cggdp / 100)

p <- cgtax %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US") %>%
  select(year, cggdp) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, cggdp / 100)) +
  geom_point(colour="blue") +
  geom_line(colour="blue") +
  scale_y_continuous(name="% of GDP", labels=scales::percent_format(accuracy = .1), limits=c(0, NA)) +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL, breaks=seq(1950, 2030, 5)) +
  gband(rec$peak_decimal, rec$trough_decimal) +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept=median(cggdp, na.rm=TRUE) / 100)) +
  annotate("text", x=1955, y=median(anno$cggdp, na.rm=TRUE) +.001, label = "median", size=2.5) +
  annotate("text", x=1987, y=.0765, label = "Federal\ntax reform", size=2.5) +
  annotate("text", x=2000.25, y=.0675, label = "Dot-com\nbubble", size=2.5) +
  annotate("text", x=2007.25, y=.0685, label = "Real estate\nbubble", size=2.5) +
  ggtitle("Capital gains as a percent of GDP",
          subtitle = "Recession periods are shaded") +
  labs(caption="Horizontal line marks median for the period shown") +
  theme_report
p
ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "cggdp_us.png"), plot = p, width=8, height=6)


# theme(
#   plot.title = element_text(),
#   plot.subtitle.title = element_text(),
#   plot.caption = element_text()
# )

# p + theme(
#   plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0)
#   )

  
```





# Construct Seegert volatility measure
```{r}
df <- censustax %>%
  filter(stabbr=="US", name=="tottax", year >= 1959) %>%
  select(year, tax=value) %>%
  left_join(gdpfy, by="year") %>% # nominal gdp
  arrange(year)

df2 <- df %>%
  pivot_longer(-year) %>%
  group_by(name) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(trend=hptrend(value, smooth=100),
         vol1=log(abs(value - trend)),
         vol2=(value - trend) / trend * 100)

df2 %>%
  filter(year >= 1970) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol2, colour=name)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="volatility (% difference from smoothed trend)",
                     breaks=seq(-20, 20, 2)) +
  ggtitle("Volatility of State Government Tax Revenue and Gross Domestic Product, United States",
          subtitle="Volatility measured as % difference from smoothed trend (HP filter)") +
  theme_bw()

# Colorado-type approach
df2 <- df %>%
  mutate(return=tax / lag(tax),
         dreturn=log(return)*100)

df2 %>%
  filter(year >= 1970) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()

```


## Introduction and motivation

### Setting the scene - year over year % change
```{r pchplot, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=8, eval=TRUE, include=TRUE}
# sd10c
#  pchplot, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=8, eval=FALSE}
# summary(sgtax.a)
irecs <- which(recessions$rec_year >= 1960)

p2 <- taxgdpus %>%
  filter(year >= 1960) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vpch, colour=name)) +
  geom_line(size=1) +
  geom_point(size=1.15) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="% change", breaks=seq(-20, 20, 2)) +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL, breaks=seq(1960, 2030, 5)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("red", "blue")) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  annotate("rect",
           xmin = recessions$peak[irecs] %>% decimal_date(),
           xmax = recessions$trough[irecs] %>% decimal_date(),
           fill = "lightgrey",
           alpha = .5, # larger alpha is darker rectangle
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf) +
  ggtitle("Year over year growth in state government tax revenue and GDP for the United States",
          subtitle="Recession periods are shaded.") +
  labs(caption="Source: U.S. Bureau of the Census and Bureau of Economic Analysis") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.caption = element_text(hjust=0, size=9)) +
  theme(legend.title = element_blank())
p2
ggsave(filename = here::here("results", "tax_gdp_us.png"), plot = p2)


```


## Ways to measure tax revenue volatility

* Past research
  + Rolling variance (Dauchy 2013)
  + Deviation from trend
    + Kwak 2013 says White 1983; Dye and McGuire 1991; Braun and Otsuka 1998; Aisen and Jose´ Veiga 2008 all used deviation from trend
    + Orthogonal Distance Regression (Kwak 2013)
     

### Selected measures for the United States




#### Rolling standard deviation


#### Deviation from trend


## Recent trends in revenue volatility


## Drivers of state revenue volatility

### The nature of national economic changes

### State economic structure

### State tax structure

#### The portfolio of state taxes

#### The structure of individual taxes


##	Implications for states in current recession and beyond

### Special characteristics of the Covid-19 recession and state tax volatility

### How much can states reduce volatility with alternative tax structures?




# APPENDICES BELOW HERE


# Project management and organization
## Deadlines and schedule

* July 3, 2020 Project start
* October 3, 2020 Provide list of external reviewers
* June 3, 2021  Draft report due (plan on sooner)
* August 3, 2021 Project end and final report due


## Links

+  [Zotero folder](https://www.zotero.org/groups/2541602/pew_tax_revenue_volatility/library)
+  [Google sheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1hSWy_7ep4RbM9lfSA-LYnDeGI1c6p9Ud0GqcSlR9xJ8/edit?usp=sharing) (barely started) to summarize important articles
+  [Google drive folder](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1fM7HIP7qrKbIdndF_wQ30WxVb1j_Cq06)
    +   Draft report: [Google docs](https://docs.google.com/document/d/13MaAnySHpE8ODvvRjraMPtR7l6GFCX_hk7qDP2KC_s8/edit?usp=sharing) is current version
    +   OLD VERSION: Draft report is stored here (latest version has highest number in parentheses)


## Goals and issues

* Ways to measure tax revenue volatility

* Recent trends in revenue volatility
  + Has tax revenue become more volatile over time?
  + If so, why?

* Drivers of state revenue volatility - to what extent driven by
  + The nature of national economic changes (e.g., characteristics of recession)?
  + State economic structure?
  + State tax structure? 
    + The portfolio of taxes? 
    + The structure of individual taxes?

*	Implications for states in current recession and beyond
  +  What does the Covid-19 recession imply for state tax volatility?
  +  How much can states reduce volatility with alternative tax structures?
    

Note: For details on references see [this](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1hSWy_7ep4RbM9lfSA-LYnDeGI1c6p9Ud0GqcSlR9xJ8/edit?usp=sharing).


## Notes to reviewers

Use renv.



## Data tasks and status
### Done

* State tax revenue from Census by tax, state, and year, 1951-2019, collapsed to the following categories (currently):
  + total taxes (tottax)
  + individual income tax (iit)
  + general sales tax (gst)
  + selective sales taxes such as tobacco, motor fuel, motor vehicle fees, alcohol… (selsalestax)
  + corporate income tax (cit)
  + severance taxes (sevtax)
  + all other taxes (othertax)
* Pew data on policy changes  
* Economic data
  + gross domestic product, by state fiscal year, 1951-2019 (still must adjust for states with fiscal years different from July 1)
  + gross domestic price index (chained), by state fiscal year, 1951-2019 (same adjustment required)
  + official NBER dates for recessions during relevant time periods
  + obtained NIPA details for description of recessions
  + obtained state-level consumption data from BEA

### TODO

* organize NIPA data to measure recessions
* organize NIPA data for consumption details
* organize state consumption data
* Possibly get data from Dauchy
* Graph pch tax vs econ
* IRS data components income
* Measures of income tax progressivity
* Similar measures sales tax?
* Consider base = revenue / rate as robustness check

## Details about the project

### Overall goals

Examine tax revenue volatility and its impacts on state governments

Provide deep and broad understanding of:

* Ways to measure tax revenue volatility
* Recent trends in revenue volatility
* Drivers of state revenue volatility
*	Implications for states in current recession and beyond

Ultimate goal: Insights that can help policymakers manage budgets


### Important sub-questions

* To what extent is volatility driven by:
    + The nature of national economic changes (e.g., characteristics of recession)?
    + State economic structure?
    + State tax structure? The portfolio of taxes? The structure of individual taxes?
* Has tax revenue become more volatile over time? If so, why?
* How much can states reduce volatility with alternative tax structures?

All have implications for states in current recession and beyond.


### Overall plan

1. Review relevant literature
2. Build database for analyzing volatility
    + Annual state tax revenue, all states, by tax
    + Auxiliary data to allow population & inflation adjustment
    + Selected other data
3. Multiple methods
    + Descriptive analytic review of history
    + Decomposition
    + Simulations of portfolio effects
    + Exploratory econometrics for certain topics


### Core data source

* Census Bureau Annual Survey of State Finances
  + State tax revenue, annual, all states, many taxes
  + Comprehensive (not just general fund)
  + Relatively consistent definitions over time and states
  + Annual period consistent with policymaking cycle
  + Includes multiple recessions (some data back to mid-1950s):
      + 1980-81, 1990, 2001, and 2007 (2020 won't be available)
      + may be able to include 1960, 1969, 1973 recessions

### Selected technical & data issues

* Tax data implicitly include embedded policy changes. Use Pew tax-change data to help answer: How much does adjusting for policy changes affect conclusions?
* How much do alternative volatility measures affect conclusions?
* Data on structure of individual taxes are limited. To extent practical, use these limited data to help answer: How does structure of individual taxes affect volatility?
  

### Topics and approaches
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| Topic                            | Methods                                      |
+==================================+==============================================+
| Ways to measure                  | - Literature review                          |
| revenue volatility               | - Conceptual differences among measures      |
|                                  | - Trends with different measures             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| Recent revenue volatility trends | - Descriptive analysis                       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| Drivers of revenue volatility    | - Descriptive analysis                       |
|   (Nature of economic change,    | - Decomposition                              |
|    State economic structure,     | - Econometric analysis                       |
|    State tax structure)          | - Simulation of alternative structures       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| Implications in current          | - Synthesis of above                         |
| recession and beyond             |                                              |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------------------+


### Comments from the August 4, 2020 methods review

* Clarify key terms with thorough definitions and describe the applied metrics, highlighting areas of standardization. Absent those details, some confusion may arise. Specifically,
    +	Convey the practical differences between the proposed metrics of revenue volatility for state and other policymakers. These metrics can vary considerably depending on the underlying data distribution, meaningfully changing what is observed as volatility. 
    + Elaborate on how revenue streams may need to be combined/collapsed for analytical tractability. If doing so, highlight the thresholds for those modification, if these differ by state or source, and if those decisions affect the results.  
    + Account for the possible interpretations of concepts like “state economic structures” and economies “at risk”, and how those choices shift the meaning of the analysis and findings. If these concepts are applied differently by state, explain how these may be standardized.  

* Ensure that the selected time frames (periods and trends) and their decision rules are transparent in the final report. Specifically: 
    + Discuss how partial recessionary periods (i.e. quarters) will be addressed since the study will generally leverage annual data, to speak to policymaker budgeting cycles/priorities.
    +	Clarify that “recent trends” may reflect multi-decade periods of fiscal management, which may not be self-evident. Explain how the bounds of “trend periods” are chosen, whether through an assessment of business cycles, regression analyses, inductive analyses or other techniques.

* Ensure consistency between this and prior Pew studies, or provide a crosswalk, particularly if the methods have been modified or augmented. Focus on what sources of uncertainty remain embedded in each measure of volatility. 
    + Assess whether correcting for tax policy adjustments changes estimated volatility. Also, since the policy adjusted time series is shorter, estimate whether a shorter series will complicate the development of robust volatility estimates using the more technical/complex approaches. 
    + Highlight — for more technical methods — how trends are being estimated, and how differences in those trends might change the interpretation of volatility, as the embedded sources of uncertainty differ (e.g. by extracting cyclicality). 







```{r tax_data, eval=TRUE}

# tax_stack <- tax2 %>%
#   rename(nominal=value) %>%
#   left_join(gdppi %>% select(year, igdppi), by="year") %>%
#   mutate(real=nominal * igdppi) %>%
#   select(-igdppi) %>%
#   pivot_longer(c(nominal, real), names_to="type")

```


```{r}
# data checking
# what to do about negative tax values?? they will become nan in logs, and we need to be robust to that
# what about zero values in the middle that look like they must be missing
# OH cit 2014 is negative (-$118)
# NM cit has pos 1951-1954, zero 1955-1966, then positive

tax_stack %>%
  filter(value <= 0) %>%
  as.data.frame()

tax_stack %>%
  filter(stabbr=="OH", name=="cit", type=="nominal")

tax_stack %>%
  filter(stabbr=="NM", name=="cit", type=="nominal")

measures %>%
  filter(stabbr=="OH", name=="cit", type=="nominal")
```


### Basic tax measures
```{r}


measures <- tax_stack %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
  mutate(lvalue=log(value))

#   nest() %>%

```



## Pew data on enacted revenue changes

*   Pew constructed estimates of the percent change in major taxes, by year, state, and major tax type for work it did in ___
*   In a 10/8/2015 email, on my request Brenna Erford <BErford@pewtrusts.org> provided the file "Boyd Data Request 100815.xlsx" to me (donald.boyd@rockinst.suny.edu), which had data underlying the project
*   Pew's short description of the data, in the "Cover" tab of the workbook, was:
    
<blockquote style="color:grey;font-size:100%;margin-left: 4em">
  <p>Volatility scores for each state’s total tax revenue and specific tax sources were calculated using the U.S. Census Bureau’s State Government Tax Collections historical data series for 1994 to 2014, accessed April 21, 2015. Data were adjusted to control for the effects of tax policy change using the National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL) State Tax Action reports for 1994 to 2013, accessed in January 2015.</p>
</blockquote>


```{r ONETIME_pew_revenue_data, eval=FALSE}
# data file Boyd Data Request 100815.xlsx
xlfn <- here::here("data", "Boyd Data Request 100815.xlsx")
sheets <- excel_sheets(xlfn)
sheets

get_pewsheet <- function(sheet){
  print(sheet)
  range <- ifelse(str_detect(sheet, "Major"), "A5:V190", "A5:V56")
  read_excel(xlfn, sheet=sheet, range=range) %>%
    mutate(sheet=sheet)
}

pewdf <- map_dfr(sheets[-1], get_pewsheet)
glimpse(pewdf)
count(pewdf, sheet)
count(pewdf, `Major Revenue Source`)

#    `Major Revenue Source`               n
#    <chr>                            <int>
#  1 Amusements                           9
#  2 Corporation                          9
#  3 Corporation net income              90
#  4 Documentary and stock transfer       6
#  5 General sales and gross receipts   135
#  6 Individual income                  123
#  7 Motor fuels                         99
#  8 Motor vehicle                       12
#  9 Occupation and business, NEC         6
# 10 Property taxes                      27
# 11 Public utilities                     9
# 12 Severance                           27
# 13 Tobacco products                     3
# 14 Total                              153

type_map <- read_delim("revsource; taxtype
Amusements; amusetax
Corporation; corptax
Corporation net income; cit
Documentary and stock transfer; doctax
General sales and gross receipts; gst
Individual income; iit
Motor fuels; mft
Motor vehicle; mvl
Occupation and business, NEC; occtax
Property taxes; proptax
Public utilities; utiltax
Severance; sevtax
Tobacco products; cigtax
Total; tottax", 
delim=";", trim_ws = TRUE)

pewlong <- pewdf %>%
  select(stname=1, revsource=2, everything()) %>%
  left_join(bdata::stcodes %>% select(stabbr, stname), by="stname") %>%
  left_join(type_map, by="revsource") %>%
  mutate(vartype=word(sheet, 3)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-c(stabbr, stname, revsource, sheet, vartype, taxtype), names_to = "year") %>%
  mutate(year=as.integer(year)) %>%
  select(stabbr, stname, sheet, vartype, revsource, taxtype, year, value) %>%
  arrange(stabbr, vartype, taxtype, year)
  
glimpse(pewlong)

# caution: US data do not have major taxes, just the total
count(pewlong, stabbr, stname)

pewlong %>% filter(stabbr=="US")
saveRDS(pewlong, here::here("data", "pewlong.rds"))


```





```{r vol_measures}
# hp_df <- function(df, vname){
#   vts <- ts(df[vname])
#   vts_hp <- hpfilter(vts, freq=NULL, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
#   df %>% mutate(trend=as.numeric(vts_hp$trend),
#                 cycle=as.numeric(vts_hp$cycle))
# }


# takes a LONG LONG time
# vol1 <- tax_real %>%
#   filter(name=="tottax") %>%
#   group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
#   arrange(year) %>%
#   hp_df(., "value")
#   



# df <- df %>%
#   f(., "iit") %>%
#   mutate(dtrend=cycle / trend * 100,
#          pch=iit / iit[match(year - 1, year)] * 100 - 100,
#          dtrend_sd10=rollsd_p(dtrend, 10),
#          pch_sd10=rollsd_p(pch, 10))


```



```{r explore}
# remove trend
df <- sgtax.a %>%
  filter(year >= 1951, stabbr=="NY", item=="T40") %>%
  select(year, stabbr, iit=value)
df %>% ht  

# iit <- ts(df$iit, start = 1951, end=2019)
# iit <- ts(df$iit)
# plot(iit)
# 
# stl(iit, s.window = "periodic")
# # hpfilter(iit, freq=NULL, type=c("lambda","frequency"),drift=FALSE)
# iit_hp <- hpfilter(iit, freq=NULL, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
# iit_hp$trend
# iit_hp$cycle

f <- function(df, vname){
  vts <- ts(df[vname])
  vts_hp <- hpfilter(vts, freq=NULL, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
  df %>% mutate(trend=as.numeric(vts_hp$trend),
                cycle=as.numeric(vts_hp$cycle))
}

df <- df %>%
  f(., "iit") %>%
  mutate(dtrend=cycle / trend * 100,
         pch=iit / iit[match(year - 1, year)] * 100 - 100,
         dtrend_sd10=rollsd_p(dtrend, 10),
         pch_sd10=rollsd_p(pch, 10))

glimpse(df)


df %>%
  select(year, iit, trend) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=c(iit, trend)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=name)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()


df %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, dtrend)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)
  
df %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, pch)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

# df %>%
#   select(year, dtrend, pch) %>%
#   pivot_longer(cols=-year) %>%
#   ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=name)) +
#   geom_line() +
#   geom_point() +
#   geom_hline(yintercept = 0)
# 
# df %>%
#   select(year, dtrend, pch) %>%
#   mutate(pch=pch - mean(pch, na.rm=TRUE)) %>%
#   pivot_longer(cols=-year) %>%
#   ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=name)) +
#   geom_line() +
#   geom_point() +
#   geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

# another change

df %>%
  select(year, dtrend_sd10, pch_sd10) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=-year) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=name)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)
 
```


## Calculate volatility measures

```{r vfunctions}
hp_df <- function(df, vname, smooth=NULL){
  vts <- ts(df[vname])
  vts_hp <- hpfilter(vts, freq=smooth, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
  df %>% 
    mutate(trend=as.numeric(vts_hp$trend),
           cycle=as.numeric(vts_hp$cycle))
}

```


```{r}
tax_stacked
count(tax_stacked, name)
tax_stacked %>% 
  filter(type=="nominal", name=="tottax") %>%
  group_by(stabbr) %>%
  summarise(firstyear=min(year),
            lastyear=max(year))  # 1951-2019

# differences from actual revenue
# prep the tax file -- determine first year of each series!!! and start there
taxprep <- tax_stacked %>%
  arrange(stabbr, name, type, year) %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
  mutate(ratio=value / value[match(year - 1,  year)],
         logpch=log(ratio) * 100,
         pch=value / value[match(year - 1,  year)] * 100 - 100)
taxprep
# note logpch warning in  stabbr = "OH", name = "cit",

# separate files with the different measures
# hpfilter all items

hp2 <- function(df, vname, smooth=NULL){
  vts <- ts(df[vname])
  vts_hp <- hpfilter(vts, freq=smooth, type=c("lambda"), drift=FALSE)
  tibble(trend=as.numeric(vts_hp$trend),
         cycle=as.numeric(vts_hp$cycle))
}

a <- proc.time()
hpall <- tax_stacked %>%
  # filter(stabbr=="OH") %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
  nest()  %>%
  mutate(hp=map(data, hp2, "value", smooth=100))
b <- proc.time() 
b - a

glimpse(hpall)

hpall %>%
  filter(stabbr=="OH", type=="nominal", name=="cit") %>%
  unnest(cols = c(data, hp))


```




# Appendix

## Colorado revenue volatility review
```{r covol}


# lambda
freq <- 1
lam <- (2 * sin(pi / freq))^(-4)  # lambda w/freq=1: 2.779035e+62


conom <- slgfin %>%
  filter(stabbr=="CO", level==2, aggvar=="osr", year %in% 1992:2017) %>%
  arrange(year)

cohp <- conom %>%
  mutate(return=value / lag(value),
         dreturn=log(return) * 100) %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "dreturn", smooth=1600) %>%
  mutate(vol=cycle)
sum(cohp$cycle)

cohp_dtrend <- conom %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "value", smooth=1600) %>%
  mutate(vol=cycle / trend * 100)

comp <- bind_rows(cohp %>% select(year, vol) %>% mutate(name="dpch"),
                  cohp_dtrend %>% select(year, vol) %>% mutate(name="dtrend"))

comp %>% 
  pivot_wider(values_from = vol)
  

cohp %>%
  mutate(vol=cycle) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="blue") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=1993:2017) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=seq(-20, 20, 2.5)) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust=0, hjust=0))

cohp %>%
  select(year, dreturn, trend) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=-year) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour=name)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

comp %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol, colour=name)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

# djb new start ----
param <- 1600

cohp <- conom %>%
  mutate(return=value / lag(value),
         dreturn=log(return) * 100) %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "dreturn", smooth=param) %>%
  mutate(vol=cycle)

cohp_dtrend <- conom %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "value", smooth=param) %>%
  mutate(vol=cycle / trend * 100)

comp <- bind_rows(cohp %>% select(year, vol) %>% mutate(name="dpch"),
                  cohp_dtrend %>% select(year, vol) %>% mutate(name="dtrend"))

p <- comp %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  mutate(name=factor(name,
                     levels=c("dpch", "dtrend"),
                     labels=c("Calculated as footnote describes",
                              "Calculated as graph is labeled"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="blue") +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL, breaks=1993:2017) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="Percent", breaks=seq(-20, 20, 5), limits=c(-15, 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~name, ncol=1) +
  ggtitle("Colorado's revenue deviation from trend calculated two ways",
          subtitle=paste0("HP filter smoothing parameter: ", param)) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust=0.5, hjust=0.5))
p



cotaxhp <- slgfin %>%
  filter(stabbr=="CO", level==2, aggvar=="tottax", year %in% 1992:2017) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(return=value / lag(value),
         dreturn=log(return) * 100) %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "dreturn", smooth=1600)

cohp %>%
  select(year, cycle) %>%
  mutate(name="osr") %>%
  bind_rows(cotaxhp %>% 
              select(year, cycle) %>%
              mutate(name="tax")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, cycle, colour=name)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

co_vol <- tax_stacked %>%
  filter(name=="tottax", type=="real", stabbr=="CO", year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  hp_df(., "value")


co_vol %>%
  mutate(vol= cycle / trend * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity")


co_vol2 <- tax_stacked %>%
  filter(name=="tottax", type=="nominal", stabbr=="CO", year %in% 1992:2017) %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(return=value / lag(value),
         dreturn=log(return) * 100) %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "dreturn")

co_vol2

co_vol2 %>%
  mutate(vol= cycle / trend * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vol)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity")

tax_stacked %>%
  filter(name=="tottax", type=="nominal", stabbr=="CO", year %in% 1992:2017) %>%
  group_by(stabbr, name, type) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(pch=value / lag(value) * 100 - 100) %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  mutate(diff=pch - median(pch))


data(package="bdata")

tmp <- slgfin %>%
  filter(stabbr=="CO", level==2, aggvar=="osr", year %in% 1992:2017)

cov3 <- tmp %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(return=value / lag(value),
         dreturn=log(return) * 100) %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017) %>%
  hp_df(., "dreturn") %>%
  filter(year %in% 1993:2017)


```



# Earlier notes



```{r pchplot_old, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=8, eval=FALSE}
# summary(sgtax.a)
irecs <- which(recessions$rec_year >= 1960)

p1 <- sgt1 %>%
  filter(year >= 1960) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, vpch)) +
  geom_line(colour="blue", size=1) +
  geom_point(colour="blue", size=1.15) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="% change", breaks=seq(-20, 20, 2)) +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL, breaks=seq(1960, 2030, 5)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  annotate("rect",
           xmin = recessions$peak[irecs] %>% decimal_date(),
           xmax = recessions$trough[irecs] %>% decimal_date(),
           fill = "lightgrey",
           alpha = .5, # larger alpha is darker rectangle
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf) +
  ggtitle("Year over year growth in state government tax revenue for the United States",
          subtitle="Recession periods are shaded.") +
  labs(caption="Source: U.S. Bureau of the Census") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.caption = element_text(hjust=0, size=9))
p1

```



```{r vplot, eval=FALSE}
# summary(sgtax.a)

irecs <- which(recessions$rec_year >= 1960)

p <- sgt1 %>%
  filter(year >= 1960) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year, sd10)) +
  geom_line(colour="blue") +
  geom_point(colour="blue", size=0.75) +
  scale_y_continuous(name="Standard deviation (%)", breaks=seq(0, 10, .5), limits=c(0, NA)) +
  scale_x_continuous(name=NULL, breaks=seq(1960, 2030, 5)) +
  annotate("rect",
           xmin = recessions$peak[irecs] %>% decimal_date(),
           xmax = recessions$trough[irecs] %>% decimal_date(),
           fill = "lightgrey",
           alpha = .5, # larger alpha is darker rectangle
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf) +
  ggtitle("Volatility in aggregate state government tax revenue for the United States",
          subtitle="Trailing 10-year standard deviation of annual % change. Recession periods are shaded.") +
  theme_bw()
p    


```
